content: A battery of e.m.f. E and internal resistance r is connected to an external resistance R the condition for maximum power transfer is
explanation:
Current in the circuit $$I = {E \over {R + r}}$$

Power delivered to the resistance R is

$$P = {I^2}\,R = {{{E^2}R} \over {{{(R + r)}^2}}}$$

It is maximum when $${{dP} \over {dR}} = 0$$

$${{dP} \over {dR}} = {E^2}\left[ {{{{{(r + R)}^2} - 2R(r + R)} \over {{{(r + R)}^4}}}} \right] = 0$$

or $${(r + R)^2} = 2R(r + R)$$

or $$R = r$$

content: A thin wire of resistance 4 $$\Omega$$ is bent to form a circle. The resistance across any diameter is
explanation:
The resistance of the total wire is 4$$\Omega$$

Here ACB (2 $$\Omega$$ and ADB (2 $$\Omega$$) are in parallel.

$$ \Rightarrow {1 \over R} = {1 \over 2} + {1 \over 2} = {2 \over 2} = 1 \Rightarrow R = 1\,\Omega $$

content: Consider a circuit where a cell of emf $$E_0$$ and internal resistance $$\mathrm{r}$$ is connected across the terminal $$\mathrm{A}$$ and $$\mathrm{B}$$ as shown in figure. The value of $$\mathrm{R}$$ for which the power generated in the circuit is maximum, is given by
explanation:
$R_{e q}=R\|R\| R=\frac{R}{3}$

$\Rightarrow r=R_{e q}=\frac{R}{3}$

$\therefore R=3 r$

content: In an experiment on a circuit as shown in the figure, the voltmeter shows 8 V reading. The resistance of the voltmeter is,
explanation:
The given circuit is

Reading of voltmeter, $$V=8 \mathrm{~V}$$

$$\therefore$$ Potential difference across resistance $$20 \Omega$$

$$V^{\prime}=10-8=2 \mathrm{~V}$$

$$\therefore$$ Current through $$20 \Omega$$ resistor,

$$I=\frac{V^{\prime}}{R_1^0}=\frac{2}{20}=\frac{1}{10} \mathrm{~A}$$

Current through $$160 \Omega$$ resistor,

$$I_2=\frac{V}{R_2}=\frac{8}{160}=\frac{1}{20} \mathrm{~A}$$

$$\therefore$$ Current through voltmeter,

$$\begin{aligned} & I_1=\left(\frac{160}{160+R_V}\right) I \\ & \Rightarrow \quad I-I_2=\left(\frac{160}{160+R_V}\right) I \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{20}=\left(\frac{160}{160+R_V}\right) \times \frac{1}{10} \\ & \therefore \quad R_V=160 \Omega \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: An amount of charge Q passes through a coil of resistance R. If the current in the coil decreases to zero at a uniform rate during time T, then the amount of heat generated in the coil will be,
explanation:
$$\therefore \quad i=-\frac{i_0}{T} t+i_0$$ .... (i)

The charge flowing through the coil is

$$\begin{aligned} Q & =\text { Area of } i-t \text { graph } \\ Q & =\frac{1}{2} \times i_0 \times T \\ \therefore \quad i_0 & =2 Q / T \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ From Eq. (i),

$$i=-\frac{1}{T} \cdot \frac{2 Q}{T} \cdot t+\frac{2 Q}{T}=\frac{2 Q}{T}\left(1-\frac{t}{T}\right)$$

$$\therefore$$ Amount of heat generated in the coil is,

$$\begin{aligned} H & =\int_0^T i^2 R d t=\int_0^T\left(\frac{2 Q}{T}\right)^2\left(1-\frac{t}{T}\right)^2 R \cdot d t \\ & =\frac{4 Q^2 R}{T^2} \cdot \int_0^T\left(1-\frac{2}{T} t+\frac{t^2}{T^2}\right) d t \\ & =\frac{4 Q^2 R}{T^2}\left[t-\frac{2}{T} \cdot \frac{t^2}{2}+\frac{1}{T^2} \cdot \frac{t^3}{3}\right]_0^T=\frac{4 Q^2 R}{3 T} \end{aligned}$$

content: A battery of emf E and internal resistance r is connected with an external resistance R as shown in the figure. The battery will act as a constant voltage source if

options.content: It will never act as a constant voltage source.

explanation:
The constant voltage source provides a constant voltage to the load resistance regardless of variations or changes, in the load resistance. For this to happen, the source must have an internal resistance which is very low as compared to the resistance of the load.

i.e., $$r < < R$$

content: The carbon resistor with colour code is shown in the figure. There is no fourth band in the resistor. The value of the resistance is
explanation:
The colours of the four bands of carbon resistors are red, yellow, orange and no fourth band.


$$\therefore$$ R = 24 $$\times$$ 103$$\Omega$$ $$\pm$$ 20%

= 24k$$\Omega$$ $$\pm$$ 20%

content: Two metal wires of identical dimensions are connected in series. If $$\sigma$$<sub>1</sub> and $$\sigma$$<sub>2</sub> are the electrical conductivities of the metal wires respectively, the effective conductivity of the combination is
explanation:
Since, both wires are identical, so they have same cross-section A and same length l. Also, in series combination of wires, effective resistance,

R = R1 + R2

$$ \Rightarrow {{(l + l)} \over {{\sigma _{eff}}A}} = {1 \over {{\sigma _1}A}} + {1 \over {{\sigma _2}A}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {2 \over {{\sigma _{eff}}}} = {1 \over {{\sigma _1}}} + {1 \over {{\sigma _2}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow {\sigma _{eff}} = {{2{\sigma _1}{\sigma _2}} \over {{\sigma _1} + {\sigma _2}}}$$

content: A galvanometer can be converted to a voltmeter of full scale deflection V<sub>0</sub> by connecting a series resistance R<sub>1</sub> and can be converted to an ammeter of full scale deflection I<sub>0</sub> by connecting a shunt resistance R<sub>2</sub>. What is the current flowing through the galvanometer at its full scale deflection?
explanation:
A galvanometer can be converted to a voltmeter of full scale deflection V0 by connecting a series resistance R1, then

$${R_{^1}} = {{{V_0}} \over {{I_g}}} - G$$

$$ \Rightarrow {V_0} = {I_g}(G + {R_1})$$ ................(i)

where, Ig = current through the galvanometer and G = resistance of galvanometer.

Similarly, a galvanometer can be converted to an ammeter by connecting a shunt resistance R2, then

$${R_2} = \left( {{{{I_g}} \over {{I_0} - {I_g}}}} \right)G$$

$$ \Rightarrow {I_g} = {I_0}\left( {{{{R_2}} \over {{G_{}} + {R_2}}}} \right)$$ ..........(ii)

where, I0 = total current.

Now, from Eq. (i), we get

$${{{V_0}} \over {{I_g}}} - {R_1} = G$$ ...................(iii)

From Eq. (ii), we get

$$G + {R_2} = {{{I_0}} \over {{I_g}}} \times {R_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$G = {{{I_0}} \over {{I_g}}} \times {R_2} - {R_2}$$ .............(iv)

From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get

$${{{V_0}} \over {{I_g}}} - {R_1} = {{{I_0}} \over {{I_g}}} \times {R_2} - {R_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${{{V_0}} \over {{I_g}}} - {{{I_0}} \over {{I_g}}} \times {R_2} = {R_1} - {R_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${I \over {{I_g}}}({V_0} - {I_0}{R_2}) = {R_1} - {R_2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $${I_g} = {{{V_0} - {I_0}{R_2}} \over {{R_1} - {R_2}}}$$

content: What will be the equivalent resistance between the terminals A and B of the infinite resistive network shown in the figure?
explanation:
For equivalent resistance, between the terminals A and B of the infinite resistance network, we redraw the given circuit as


Now the 2R and $${{RX} \over {X + R}}$$ is in series, so

$$X = 2R + {{RX} \over {R + X}}$$

X2$$ - $$ $$2RX - 2{R^2} = 0$$ $$ \Rightarrow $$ $$X = {{2R \pm \sqrt {4{R^2} - 4(1)( - 2{R^2})} } \over 2}$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ $$X = {{2R \pm \sqrt {12{R^2}} } \over 2}$$ = $${{2R \pm 2\sqrt {3R} } \over 2} = (1 \pm \sqrt 3 )R$$

$$ \Rightarrow $$ X = $$(1 + \sqrt 3 )R$$

content: Five identical capacitors, of capacitance 20$$\mu$$F each, are connected to a battery of 150V, in a combination as shown in the diagram. What is the total amount of charge stored?
explanation:
The given circuit shows a balanced Wheatstone bridge. Now, the circuit becomes


In the branch AB, both capacitor are arranged in the series combination. Hence, its equivalent capacitance is given by

$${1 \over {{C_{AB}}}} = {1 \over {20}} + {1 \over {20}} = {2 \over {20}} = {1 \over {10}}$$

$${C_{AB}} = 10\mu F$$

Similarly, in branch CD,

$${C_{CD}} = 10\mu F$$

Now, CAB and CCD are connected in parallel combination. Hence, the equivalent capacitance of the circuit,

$${C_{eq}} = 10 + 10 = 20\mu F$$

We know that, charge $$Q = {C_{eq}}V$$

$$Q = 20 \times {10^{ - 6}} \times 150V$$

Amount of charge stored $$Q = 3 \times {10^{ - 3}}C$$

content: What is the current I shown in the given circuit?
explanation:
According to the question,


Total resistance of the given circuit

$${R_{eq}} = 2R$$

Now, circuit


$$ \therefore $$ $$I = {{V/2R} \over 8}$$ ($$ \because $$ $${I_1} = V/2R$$)

So, current I in the circuit $$I = {V \over {16R}}$$ $$ \because $$ $$\left( {I = {{{I_1}} \over 8}} \right)$$

content: When the value of R in the balanced Wheatstone bridge, shown in the figure, is increased from 5$$\Omega $$ to 7$$\Omega $$, the value of S has to be increased by 3$$\Omega $$ in order to maintain the balance. What is the initial values of S?
explanation:
According to the balance condition of Wheatstone bridge,

In the first case,

$${P \over Q} = {R \over S}$$

$${P \over Q} = {5 \over S}$$ ....(i)

In the second case

$${P \over Q} = {7 \over {S + 3}}$$ ... (ii)

From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get

$${5 \over S} = {7 \over {S + 3}}$$

$$5(S + 3) = 7S$$

$$5S + 15 = 7S$$

$$2S = 15$$

$$S = 7.5\Omega $$

content: A capacitor of capacitance C is connected in series with a resistance R and DC source of emf E through a key. The capacitor starts charging when the key is closed. By the time the capacitor has been fully charged, what amount of energy is dissipated in the resistance R?
explanation:
We know that, energy stored in the capacitor

= $${1 \over 2}C{E^2}$$

and energy supplied by the source of emf = CE2

$$ \therefore $$ Energy dissipated in resistance R

= Energy supplied by the source of emf E $$-$$ Energy stored in the capacitor

= $$C{E^2} - {1 \over 2}C{E^2}$$

$$ = \left( {1 - {1 \over 2}} \right)C{E^2} = {1 \over 2}C{E^2}$$

content: Four resistors, 100$$\Omega $$, 200$$\Omega $$, 300$$\Omega $$ and 400$$\Omega $$ are connected to form four sides of a square. The resistors can be connected in any order. What is the maximum possible equivalent resistance across the diagonal of the square?
explanation:
For maximum equivalent resistance across the diagonal of the square, the given resistors connected as


Resistance of PQR arm, R1 = 300 + 200$$\Omega $$ = 500$$\Omega $$

Resistance of PSR arm, R2 = 400 + 100$$\Omega $$ = 500$$\Omega $$

The equivalent resistance between P and R.

$${1 \over {{R_{eq}}}} = {1 \over {{R_1}}} + {1 \over {{R_2}}}$$

$$ = {1 \over {500}} + {1 \over {500}} = {{1 + 1} \over {500}}$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${R_{eq}} = {{500} \over 2} = 250\Omega $$

content: What will be current through the 200$$\Omega $$ resistor in the given circuit, a long time after the switch K is made on?
explanation:
In steady state, arm having capacitors does not flow current. So, we can neglect them.

$$ \therefore $$ The given circuit reduces to


$${R_{net}} = 200 + 400 = 600\Omega $$

$$ \therefore $$ Current in circuit,

$$I = {V \over R} = {6 \over {600}}$$ = 0.01 A = 10mA

content: An electric bulb, a capacitor, a battery and a switch are all in series in a circuit. How does the intensity of light vary when the switch is turn on?

options.content: gradually increases for sometime and then becomes steady
sharply rises initially and then gradually decreases
gradually increases for sometime and then gradually decreaes.

explanation: Initially, there will be no voltage drop across capacitor, so intensity of bulb will rise sharply and gradually voltage drop across capacitor will increase as a result voltage drop across bulb decreases, so intensity of bulb will decreases.

content: Six wires, each of resistance r, are connected so as to form a tetrahedron. The equivalent resistance of the combination when current enters through one corner and leaves through some other corner is
explanation:
Six wires each of resistance r from a tetrahedron as shown in the following figure


The equivalent circuit of this tetrahedron


It is circuit of Wheatstone the equivalent resistance of upper circuit

$${1 \over R} = {1 \over {2r}} + {1 \over {2r}} = {2 \over {2r}} = {1 \over r}$$

R = r

It will in parallel with outer resistance

$${1 \over {{R_{eq}}}} = {1 \over r} + {1 \over r} = {2 \over r}$$

$${R_{eq}} = {r \over 2}$$

content:
Consider the circuit shown in the figure.


The value of the resistance X for which the thermal power generated in it is practically independent of small variation of its resistance is
explanation:
For above circuit

$${1 \over {R'}} = {1 \over R} + {1 \over X} = {{R + X} \over {RX}}$$

$$R' = {{RX} \over {R + X}}$$

The current in the circuit

$$i = {E \over {R + R'}} = {E \over {\left( {R + {{RX} \over {R + X}}} \right)}}$$

$${V_{RX}} = {{E.{{RX} \over {R + X}}} \over {\left( {R + {{RX} \over {R + X}}} \right)}} = {{EX} \over {R + 2X}}$$

Power dissipated in the circuit

$${P_X} = {{V_{RX}^2} \over X} = {{{E^2}.\,{X^2}} \over {X{{(R + 2X)}^2}}}$$

$$ = {{{E^2}X} \over {{{(R + 2X)}^2}}}$$

$${{d{P_X}} \over {dX}} = {E^2}{{(R - 2X)} \over {{{(R + 2X)}^3}}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow d{P_X} = {{{E^2}(R - 2X)} \over {{{(R + 2X)}^3}}}.\,dX$$

(dPX) will be zero for all (dX) if

$$x = {r \over 2}\left[ {d{P_X} = {{{E^2}\left( {R - 2 \times {R \over 2}} \right)} \over {{{\left( {R + 2 \times {R \over 2}} \right)}^3}}}} \right]$$

content: Consider the circuit shown in the figure where all the resistances are of magnitude 1 k$$\Omega$$. If the current in the extreme right resistance X is 1 mA, the potential difference between A and B is
explanation: The potential between points A and B VAB = 34 volt

content: Consider the circuit given here. The potential difference VBC between the points B and C is
explanation:
From circuit the current,

$$I = {E \over R}$$

The equivalent resistance of above circuit

= 3 + 2 + 1

= 6 k$$\Omega$$

= 6 $$ \times $$ 103 $$\Omega$$

E = 3 volt

$$I = {3 \over {6 \times {{10}^3}}}$$

= 0.5 $$ \times $$ 10$$-$$3 A

Potential,

$${V_{AD}} = iR = 0.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 3 \times {10^3}$$

= 1.5 V

Charge,

$${1 \over C} = {1 \over 1} + {1 \over 2} = {3 \over 2}$$

C $$ = {2 \over 3}$$

Q = C . VAD

$${2 \over 3} \times 1.5$$

= 1 $$\mu$$C

Applying KVL (Kirchhoff's Voltage Law) from B to C

$${V_B} - 0.5 \times {10^{ - 3}} \times 2 \times {10^3} + {1 \over 2} = {V_C}$$

$${V_B} - {V_C} = 1 - {1 \over 2} = 0.5$$ V

content: Two wires of same radius having lengths l<sub>1</sub> and l<sub>2</sub> and resistivities $${{\rho _1}}$$ and $${{\rho _2}}$$ are connected in series. The equivalent resistivity will be
explanation:
$$R = {{\rho l} \over A}$$

where, $$\rho $$ = resistivity

l = length of the resistance wire

A = area of cross-section

When the wires are connected in series, then

$$R = {R_1} + {R_2}$$

$$R = {{{\rho _1}{l_1}} \over {{A_1}}} + {{{\rho _1}{l_2}} \over {{A_2}}}$$

$$ = {{{\rho _1}{l_1}} \over A} + {{{\rho _2}{l_2}} \over A}$$ $$ \because $$ $$({A_1} = {A_2})$$

$${\rho _{eq}}$$ = resistivity of combination

$$l = {l_1} + {l_2}$$

$$A = A$$

Thus, $${\rho _{eq}}.{{{l_1} + {l_2}} \over A} = {{{\rho _1}{l_1} + {\rho _2}{l_2}} \over A}$$

$${\rho _{eq}} = {{{\rho _1}{l_1} + {\rho _2}{l_2}} \over {{l_1} + {l_2}}}$$

content: Two equal resistances, 400 $$\Omega$$ each, are connected in series with a 8 V battery. If the resistance of first one increases by 0.5%, the change required in the resistance of the second one in order to keep the potential difference across it unaltered is to
explanation:
In series combination,

R = R1 + R2

Same current will pass in R1 and R2. According to question, R1 is increased by 0.5%.

$$ \therefore $$ Increment in the resistance

= $${{0.5} \over {100}} \times 400$$

= 2.0 $$\Omega$$

So to keep the potential unchanged in second resistance the change required will 2.0 $$\Omega$$ increment.

content: The effective resistance between A and B in the figure is $${7 \over {12}}\Omega $$ if each side of the cube has 1$$\Omega$$ resistance. The effective resistance between the same two points, when the link AB is removed, is
explanation:
Let when link AB is removed, x is an equivalent of remaining part of cube without link.

AB and rest part will be in parallel, so according to question,

$${7 \over {12}} = {{{R_1}{R_2}} \over {{R_1} + R}} = {{1(x)} \over {1 + x}} = {x \over {1 + x}}$$

$${7 \over {12}} = {x \over {1 + x}}$$

$$12x = 7 + 7x$$

$$12x - 7x = 7$$

$$5x = 7$$

$$x = {7 \over 5}\Omega $$

content: Two wires each of same material and same length have 2 L 1 ratio of their radii. If the first wire has a resistance of 2 $$\Omega$$ then what will be the equivalent resistance of the two wires when they are connected in series?
explanation:
$$R = {{\rho l} \over A} = {{\rho l} \over {\pi {r^2}}}$$

$$\therefore$$ $$R \propto {1 \over {{r^2}}}$$ where $$\rho$$, $$l$$ constant.

$$\therefore$$ Here $${R_1} = 2\,\Omega $$,

$${{{R_2}} \over {{R_1}}} = {\left( {{{{r_1}} \over {{r_2}}}} \right)^2} \Rightarrow {R_2} = {\left( {{2 \over 1}} \right)^2} \times 2 = 8\,\Omega $$

$$\therefore$$ When they are in series, equivalent resistance

$$R = {R_1} + {R_2} = 10\,\Omega $$

content: A 60 W - 220 V bulb is connected in series with another bulb 40 W - 220 V. Which bulb will give more illuminance ?
explanation:
$$P = {{{V^2}} \over R}$$

For a given V

$$R \propto {1 \over P}$$

$$\therefore$$ $${R_{40}} > {R_{60}}$$

In series, the current remains the same.

$$\therefore$$ $$P = {l^2}R$$

As $${R_{40}} > {R_{60}}$$, the bulb 40 W - 220 V will given more illuminance.

content: In the circuit shown in the figure all the resistance are identical and each has the value r$$\Omega $$. The equivalent resistance of the combination between the points A and B will remain unchanged even when the following pairs of points marked in the figure are connected through a resistance R.
explanation:
The circuit diagram is as shown below,


According to question, when given pair of point in options are connected through a resistance R, then equivalent resistance between point A and B (RAB) remains unchanged. It is only possible when current does not flows through resistance R and circuit become Wheatstone bridge as shown in the figure below,


At the balanced condition, $${P \over Q} = {R \over S}$$

The current flow in CD branch will be zero. Now by checking each option,

from option (a), circuit is,


Now, $$ \because $$ $${P \over Q} = {R \over S} \Rightarrow {{2r} \over {4r}} = {r \over {2r}} \Rightarrow {1 \over 2} = {1 \over 2}$$

Hence, option (a) is correct.

From option (b), circuit is


Now, $${P \over Q} = {R \over S} \Rightarrow {{3r} \over {3r}} \ne {r \over {2r}}$$

So, option (b) is also incorrect.

From option (c) circuit is,


Now, $${P \over Q} = {R \over S} \Rightarrow {{4r} \over {2r}} = {{2r} \over r} \Rightarrow {2 \over 1} = {2 \over 1}$$

So, option (c) is also correct.

From option (d), circuit is


Now, $${P \over Q} = {R \over S} \Rightarrow {{4r} \over {2r}} \ne {r \over {2r}}$$

So, option (d) is also incorrect.

Hence, option (a) and (c) are correct.

content: A non-zero current passes through the galvanometer G shown in the circuit when the key K is closed and its value does not change when the key is opened. Then, which of the following statement(s) is/are true?

options.content: the current through the galvanometer is 40 mA.
After the key is closed, the current through the 200$$\Omega$$ resistor is same as the current through the 300$$\Omega$$ resister.

explanation:
$$ \therefore $$$${I_G} = {{10} \over {100 + G}}$$

$$300{I_3} = {{10} \over {100 + G}}G$$

$${I_3} = {G \over {30}}\left[ {{1 \over {100 + G}}} \right]$$

$$ \therefore $$ $${I_3} + {I_G} = {1 \over {(100 + G)}}\left[ {10 + {G \over {30}}} \right] = {{(300 + G)} \over {(100 + G) \times 30}}$$

According to the problem,

$$10 = \left( {{{200} \over 3} + {{300G} \over {300 + G}}} \right)\left[ {{{(300 + G)} \over {(100 + G)30}}} \right]$$ [$$ \because $$ V = RI]

$$10 = {{60000 + 1100G} \over 3} \times {1 \over {100 + G}} \times {1 \over {30}}$$

$$ \Rightarrow 900(100 + G) = 60000 + 1100G$$

$$ \Rightarrow 30000 = 200G$$

$$ \therefore $$ $$G = 150\Omega $$

$${I_G} = {{10} \over {100 + 150}} = {{10} \over {250}}$$ = 40 mA

As $${{200} \over {100}} = {{300} \over {150}}$$

So, $${I_{200}} = {I_{300}}$$
